organicVisitors.title: Organic Visitors
organicVisitors.description: Unique users who visited your site through organic (non-paid) search results.
organicVisitors.details: This metric counts individual people, not page views. If one person visits your site 5 times, they count as 1 organic visitor. This helps you understand how many actual people are discovering your content through search engines. A higher number indicates better SEO performance and content visibility.

totalPageViews.title: Total Page Views
totalPageViews.description: The total number of pages viewed on your site during the selected period.
totalPageViews.details: Every time a user loads a page, it counts as a page view - including repeated views of the same page. This metric helps you understand overall engagement and content consumption. Compare with visitors to calculate pages per session and identify how deeply users explore your site.

avgSessionDuration.title: Average Session Duration
avgSessionDuration.description: The average amount of time users spend on your site during a single session.
avgSessionDuration.details: A session starts when a user arrives on your site and ends after 30 minutes of inactivity or at midnight. Longer session durations typically indicate engaging content that keeps visitors interested. Use this metric alongside bounce rate to evaluate content quality and user experience.

sessions.title: Sessions
sessions.description: Total number of individual browsing sessions on your site.
sessions.details: A session groups user interactions within a time frame. One user can have multiple sessions. Sessions help you understand traffic patterns and user behavior. Compare sessions with users to see how often people return to your site, and with page views to calculate pages per session.

totalClicks.title: Total Clicks
totalClicks.description: The number of times users clicked on your site in Google search results.
totalClicks.details: This data comes from Google Search Console and shows actual clicks from search results to your website. A click means someone saw your listing and chose to visit your site. Increasing clicks while maintaining or improving CTR indicates growing search presence and compelling search snippets.

totalImpressions.title: Total Impressions
totalImpressions.description: How many times your site appeared in Google search results.
totalImpressions.details: An impression is counted each time your URL appears in search results, whether or not it was scrolled into view. High impressions with low clicks may indicate a need to improve your title tags and meta descriptions. This metric helps you understand your overall search visibility.

avgCtr.title: Average CTR (Click-Through Rate)
avgCtr.description: The percentage of impressions that resulted in a click.
avgCtr.details: CTR is calculated as (Clicks ÷ Impressions) × 100. A higher CTR means your search listings are compelling users to click. Typical CTR varies by position - top results average 3-5% CTR, while lower positions see much less. Improving title tags and meta descriptions can boost CTR.

avgPosition.title: Average Position
avgPosition.description: Your average ranking position in Google search results.
avgPosition.details: Position 1 is the top of search results. This is averaged across all queries your site appears for. A lower number is better. Position affects visibility and CTR significantly - the top 3 positions receive the majority of clicks. Track position changes to measure SEO progress over time.

url.title: Page URL
url.description: The full address of the scanned page on your website.
url.details: Each page on your site is scanned individually. The URL helps you identify which page has issues and navigate directly to it. Pages are discovered from your sitemap, WordPress API, or by crawling internal links.

status.title: HTTP Status Code
status.description: The server's response code when the page was requested.
status.details: 200 means the page loaded successfully. 301/302 indicate redirects. 404 means the page was not found. 500+ codes indicate server errors. Search engines use status codes to understand page availability - non-200 pages may be dropped from the index.

ttfb.title: Time To First Byte (TTFB)
ttfb.description: How long it takes for the server to send the first byte of data.
ttfb.details: TTFB measures server responsiveness. A good TTFB is under 200ms. High TTFB can indicate slow hosting, unoptimized server code, or missing caching. It directly impacts all other loading metrics since the browser can't start rendering until data arrives.

psi.title: PageSpeed Insights Score
psi.description: Google's overall performance score for this page (0-100).
psi.details: This score comes from Google PageSpeed Insights and reflects real-world loading performance. Scores 90+ are considered good, 50-89 need improvement, and below 50 are poor. The score factors in multiple metrics including LCP, CLS, FCP, and Total Blocking Time.

lcp.title: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)
lcp.description: How long it takes for the largest visible element to fully render.
lcp.details: LCP measures perceived load speed - when users see the main content appear. Google considers under 2.5 seconds good, 2.5-4.0 seconds needs improvement, and over 4.0 seconds poor. Common causes of slow LCP include unoptimized images, slow server response, and render-blocking resources.

cls.title: Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)
cls.description: How much the page layout shifts unexpectedly during loading.
cls.details: CLS measures visual stability. A score under 0.1 is good, 0.1-0.25 needs improvement, and above 0.25 is poor. Layout shifts happen when images load without dimensions, fonts swap, or ads inject content. High CLS frustrates users as they may accidentally click the wrong element.

issues.details: Issues include missing meta tags, heading problems, image optimization opportunities, security headers, and more. Each issue is categorized by severity - errors need immediate attention, warnings should be addressed soon, and info items are suggestions for improvement.

suggestions.fixBrokenResources: Fix or remove broken resource links (images, scripts, stylesheets)
suggestions.fixServerErrors: Investigate server errors (5xx) - check your hosting and server logs
suggestions.improvePageSpeed: Optimize images, reduce JavaScript, and enable caching to improve page speed
suggestions.improveLcp: Optimize the largest visible element - preload hero images, use CDN, reduce server response time
suggestions.improveCls: Set explicit width/height on images and ads, avoid inserting content above existing content
suggestions.improveInp: Reduce JavaScript execution time, break up long tasks, and use web workers for heavy processing
suggestions.fixRedirectChain: Shorten redirect chains by updating links to point directly to the final destination URL
suggestions.fixRedirectLoop: Break the redirect loop by reviewing your server configuration, .htaccess rules, or redirect plugins
suggestions.fixBrokenInternalLink: Update or remove the broken link, or set up a 301 redirect from the missing page to a relevant live page

whatIsIt.siteUnreachable: This check verifies whether your website is accessible and responding to requests. If the site is unreachable, no further audit checks can be performed. This could indicate server downtime, DNS issues, or network problems.
whatIsIt.auditFailed: The audit process encountered an unexpected error while analyzing your website. This typically means something went wrong during the scanning process rather than an issue with your website itself.
whatIsIt.ttfbCritical: Time to First Byte (TTFB) measures how long it takes for the server to send back the first byte of data after a request. A critically slow TTFB (over 3 seconds) means visitors experience significant delays before any content starts loading, which hurts both user experience and search rankings.
whatIsIt.ttfbSlow: Time to First Byte (TTFB) measures server responsiveness. A slow TTFB (over 1.5 seconds) indicates the server takes longer than recommended to begin responding, which can negatively affect page load times and SEO rankings.
whatIsIt.ttfbGood: Time to First Byte (TTFB) measures how quickly your server responds to requests. Your server is responding quickly, which contributes to fast page loads and good user experience.
whatIsIt.htmlTooLarge: This check measures the size of the raw HTML document sent from the server. A very large HTML file (over 500KB) slows down page loading, increases bandwidth usage, and can negatively impact mobile users with limited data plans.
whatIsIt.htmlLarge: This check measures your HTML document size. A moderately large document (over 200KB) may affect loading performance, especially on slower connections. Smaller HTML files load faster and are parsed more efficiently by browsers.
whatIsIt.htmlSizeGood: Your HTML document size is within a healthy range, meaning the browser can download and parse it quickly. This contributes to fast page rendering.
whatIsIt.tooManyInlineScripts: Inline scripts are JavaScript code embedded directly in the HTML. Having too many (over 10) bloats the HTML document, prevents caching of script code, and can block page rendering.
whatIsIt.inlineScriptsOk: The number of inline scripts on your page is within an acceptable range, meaning they shouldn't significantly impact loading performance.
whatIsIt.tooManyScripts: External scripts are JavaScript files loaded from URLs. Too many (over 20) create excessive HTTP requests, increase page load time, and may cause render-blocking delays.
whatIsIt.manyScripts: A relatively high number of external scripts (over 10) may slow down page loading. Each script requires an HTTP request and may block rendering unless loaded asynchronously.
whatIsIt.scriptsOk: The number of external scripts is within an acceptable range and shouldn't cause significant performance issues.
whatIsIt.tooManyStylesheets: External stylesheets require additional HTTP requests and can block page rendering. Having too many (over 10) delays the time until your page becomes visually complete.
whatIsIt.stylesheetsOk: Your stylesheet count is reasonable and shouldn't cause rendering delays.
whatIsIt.imagesNotLazy: Lazy loading delays the loading of images that are not visible on screen until the user scrolls to them. Without lazy loading, all images load at once, increasing initial page load time and wasting bandwidth.
whatIsIt.lazyLoadingGood: Your images use lazy loading correctly, meaning offscreen images load only when needed. This improves initial page load performance.
whatIsIt.imagesNoDimensions: When images lack explicit width and height attributes, the browser doesn't know how much space to reserve for them. This causes layout shifts as images load, leading to a poor Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) score.
whatIsIt.imagesNotNextGen: Next-generation image formats like WebP and AVIF provide superior compression compared to traditional formats (JPEG, PNG, GIF). They produce smaller file sizes at the same visual quality, which means faster page loads and less bandwidth usage.
whatIsIt.imagesNextGenGood: Your images are using modern next-gen formats like WebP or AVIF, which provide better compression and smaller file sizes than traditional formats.
whatIsIt.imagesTooLarge: Some images on this page have very large file sizes (over 500KB). Large images are one of the biggest contributors to slow page loads, especially on mobile devices with limited bandwidth.
whatIsIt.imagesLargeWarning: Some images on this page exceed 200KB. While not critical, these images could be further optimized to improve load times, especially for users on slower connections.
whatIsIt.imagesSizeGood: All images on this page are well-optimized in terms of file size, contributing to fast page loads.
whatIsIt.noCompression: Server-side compression (gzip or brotli) reduces the size of transferred data by 60-80%. Without compression, browsers must download much larger files, significantly slowing page loads.
whatIsIt.compressionEnabled: Your server is compressing responses, which reduces transfer sizes and improves load times.
whatIsIt.noCacheHeaders: Cache-Control headers tell browsers how long to store resources locally. Without them, browsers may re-download resources on every visit, wasting bandwidth and slowing page loads.
whatIsIt.cacheHeadersGood: Your cache headers are properly configured, allowing browsers to store and reuse resources efficiently.
whatIsIt.renderBlockingCSS: Render-blocking stylesheets prevent the browser from displaying any content until all CSS files are downloaded and parsed. Multiple blocking stylesheets increase the time until the page is visible.
whatIsIt.noTitle: The page title (<title> tag) is the most prominent text shown in search engine results. Without it, search engines may use random text from the page, and users won't see a meaningful title in their browser tab or search results.
whatIsIt.titleTooShort: A page title under 30 characters may not provide enough context for search engines or users. Short titles can lead to lower click-through rates in search results and reduced SEO effectiveness.
whatIsIt.titleTooLong: A page title over 60 characters will be truncated in search engine results, meaning users may not see the full title. This can reduce click-through rates and SEO effectiveness.
whatIsIt.titleGood: Your page title is within the optimal length range (30-60 characters), which means it displays well in search results and provides sufficient context.
whatIsIt.noMetaDescription: The meta description is the summary text shown under your page title in search results. Without one, search engines will auto-generate a snippet from your page content, which may not represent your page effectively.
whatIsIt.metaDescriptionShort: A meta description under 120 characters doesn't fully utilize the space available in search results. A longer description provides more context and can improve click-through rates.
whatIsIt.metaDescriptionLong: A meta description over 160 characters will be truncated in search results. The excess text won't be visible, which can cut off important information.
whatIsIt.metaDescriptionGood: Your meta description length is optimal (120-160 characters), providing enough detail for search results without being truncated.
whatIsIt.noH1: The H1 heading is the main title visible on your page and signals to search engines what the page is about. Without an H1, search engines have less context about your content, which can hurt rankings.
whatIsIt.multipleH1: Having more than one H1 heading can confuse search engines about the page's primary topic. Best practice is to use exactly one H1 per page, with H2-H6 for subtopics.
whatIsIt.h1Good: Your page has exactly one H1 heading, which correctly signals the main topic of the page to search engines.
whatIsIt.noH2: H2 headings create a clear content structure and help both users and search engines understand the organization of your page. Without H2s, your content lacks visible hierarchy.
whatIsIt.headingStructureGood: Your heading hierarchy is well-structured, helping search engines and users understand the content organization of your page.
whatIsIt.noCanonical: The canonical link tag tells search engines which URL is the 'official' version of a page. Without it, duplicate content across different URLs can dilute your SEO ranking power.
whatIsIt.canonicalGood: Your canonical tag is properly set, helping search engines understand which URL should be indexed and preventing duplicate content issues.
whatIsIt.missingOG: Open Graph meta tags (og:title, og:description, og:image) control how your page appears in social media shares. Without them, social platforms may display incorrect or unattractive previews of your content.
whatIsIt.ogTagsGood: Your Open Graph tags are present, ensuring your pages display attractive and accurate previews when shared on social media.
whatIsIt.imagesNoAlt: Alt text describes images for screen readers used by visually impaired users and for search engines that cannot 'see' images. Missing alt text hurts both accessibility and image SEO.
whatIsIt.allImagesHaveAlt: All your images have alt text, which is essential for accessibility and helps search engines understand your visual content.
whatIsIt.noStructuredData: Structured data (JSON-LD) provides machine-readable information about your content to search engines. It can enable rich results like star ratings, FAQs, and breadcrumbs in search results.
whatIsIt.structuredDataFound: Your page includes structured data (JSON-LD), which helps search engines understand your content and can enable rich search result features.
whatIsIt.noLangAttribute: The HTML lang attribute tells browsers and screen readers what language your page is in. Without it, assistive technologies may not pronounce content correctly, and search engines may not serve your page to the right audience.
whatIsIt.langAttributeGood: Your HTML lang attribute is properly set, helping browsers and screen readers handle your content correctly.
whatIsIt.fewInternalLinks: Internal links help users navigate your site and help search engines discover and understand relationships between your pages. Very few internal links (less than 3) may indicate poor site structure.
whatIsIt.internalLinksGood: Your page has a good number of internal links, supporting site navigation and SEO link equity distribution.
whatIsIt.noFavicon: A favicon is the small icon shown in browser tabs, bookmarks, and history. Without one, your site looks unprofessional and is harder to identify among multiple open tabs.
whatIsIt.faviconGood: Your site has a favicon, making it easily identifiable in browser tabs and bookmarks.
whatIsIt.metaRobotsNoindex: The noindex directive tells search engines not to include this page in their search results. This means the page will be invisible to anyone searching on Google, Bing, or other search engines.
whatIsIt.metaRobotsNofollow: The nofollow directive tells search engines not to follow any links on this page. This means links on this page won't pass SEO value to their destinations, which can affect your site's internal linking structure.
whatIsIt.metaRobotsGood: No blocking robots directives were found, meaning search engines are free to index this page and follow its links.
whatIsIt.robotsTxtBlocksAll: Your robots.txt file contains a rule that blocks all search engine crawlers (Disallow: /). This prevents every page on your site from being indexed and appearing in search results.
whatIsIt.wpSearchEngineDiscouraged: WordPress has a built-in setting called 'Discourage search engines from indexing this site.' When enabled, it adds a noindex meta tag to every page, preventing all pages from appearing in search results.
whatIsIt.wpSearchEngineVisible: Your WordPress search engine visibility setting allows indexing, so search engines can find and display your pages in search results.
whatIsIt.robotsTxtFound: Your robots.txt file is accessible, which helps search engines understand which parts of your site they can crawl.
whatIsIt.noRobotsTxt: The robots.txt file guides search engine crawlers on which pages to crawl or skip. Without one, crawlers will attempt to access every URL on your site, which may not be desirable.
whatIsIt.robotsTxtError: There was an error accessing your robots.txt file. This could mean it's misconfigured or temporarily unavailable, which affects how search engines crawl your site.
whatIsIt.sitemapInRobotsTxt: Your robots.txt file references your sitemap URL, making it easy for search engines to find and process your sitemap.
whatIsIt.sitemapFound: An XML sitemap was found on your site, helping search engines discover and prioritize your pages for indexing.
whatIsIt.sitemapInvalid: Your sitemap file exists but appears to contain invalid XML or incorrect formatting. An invalid sitemap may be ignored by search engines, reducing their ability to discover your pages.
whatIsIt.noSitemap: No XML sitemap was found. Sitemaps help search engines discover and prioritize your pages, especially for larger sites or pages with few incoming links.
whatIsIt.sitemapError: There was an error checking your sitemap. This could indicate the sitemap is inaccessible or malformed.
whatIsIt.redirectsOk: Your URL redirects are properly configured, ensuring users and search engines reach the correct pages.
whatIsIt.httpsEnabled: Your site uses HTTPS encryption, which protects data transmitted between your visitors and your server. HTTPS is also a confirmed Google ranking factor.
whatIsIt.noHttps: HTTPS encrypts data between your visitors and your server, protecting sensitive information. Google treats HTTPS as a ranking signal, so not having it can hurt your SEO and user trust.
whatIsIt.hstsEnabled: HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS) is enabled, telling browsers to always use HTTPS when connecting to your site, even if a user types HTTP.
whatIsIt.noHsts: HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) tells browsers to always use HTTPS. Without it, users could be vulnerable to downgrade attacks where a secure connection is intercepted and redirected to HTTP.
whatIsIt.xFrameOptionsSet: X-Frame-Options is configured, protecting your site from clickjacking attacks where your pages could be embedded in malicious frames.
whatIsIt.noXFrameOptions: The X-Frame-Options header prevents your site from being embedded in frames on other websites (clickjacking). Without it, attackers could trick users into interacting with your site unknowingly.
whatIsIt.contentTypeOptionsSet: X-Content-Type-Options is set to nosniff, preventing browsers from MIME-type sniffing which could lead to security vulnerabilities.
whatIsIt.noContentTypeOptions: The X-Content-Type-Options header prevents browsers from guessing the content type of files. Without it, a browser might interpret a file differently than intended, potentially executing malicious code.
whatIsIt.cspSet: Content Security Policy (CSP) is configured, limiting which resources can load on your pages and helping prevent cross-site scripting (XSS) attacks.
whatIsIt.noCsp: Content Security Policy (CSP) limits which external resources (scripts, styles, images) can load on your pages. Without it, your site is more vulnerable to cross-site scripting (XSS) and data injection attacks.
whatIsIt.xssProtectionSet: The X-XSS-Protection header is set, providing a basic layer of protection against reflected cross-site scripting attacks in older browsers.
whatIsIt.referrerPolicySet: A Referrer-Policy header is configured, controlling how much URL information is shared when navigating from your site to other sites.
whatIsIt.noReferrerPolicy: The Referrer-Policy header controls what referrer information is sent when users click links on your site. Without it, full URL details (including query parameters) may be leaked to third-party sites.
whatIsIt.permissionsPolicySet: A Permissions-Policy header is configured, controlling which browser features (camera, microphone, geolocation) your site can use.
whatIsIt.noPermissionsPolicy: The Permissions-Policy header controls which browser features (camera, microphone, geolocation, etc.) your website can access. Without it, embedded third-party content could access sensitive device features.
whatIsIt.mixedContent: Mixed content occurs when an HTTPS page loads resources (images, scripts, stylesheets) over insecure HTTP. This weakens security, may cause browser warnings, and can block some resources from loading.
whatIsIt.noMixedContent: Your HTTPS page loads all resources securely - no insecure HTTP resources were found.
whatIsIt.noViewport: The viewport meta tag controls how your page is displayed on mobile devices. Without it, mobile browsers will render the page at desktop width and scale it down, making it difficult to read and interact with.
whatIsIt.viewportGood: Your viewport meta tag is properly configured for responsive design on mobile devices.
whatIsIt.viewportNoDeviceWidth: Your viewport tag doesn't include width=device-width, which means the page may not properly adapt to different screen sizes. This can cause content to appear too wide or too narrow on mobile devices.
whatIsIt.zoomDisabled: Disabling pinch-to-zoom (user-scalable=no or maximum-scale=1) prevents users from enlarging text and images. This creates accessibility barriers for users with low vision who need to zoom in to read content.
whatIsIt.fixedWidthElements: Elements with fixed pixel widths don't adapt to different screen sizes. On mobile devices, this can cause horizontal scrolling, cut-off content, or overlapping elements.
whatIsIt.noResponsiveImages: Images without responsive srcset attributes serve the same size to all devices. Mobile users may download unnecessarily large images, wasting bandwidth, while high-resolution displays may show blurry images.
whatIsIt.responsiveImagesGood: Your images use responsive techniques (srcset or similar), serving appropriate sizes for different devices and screen resolutions.
whatIsIt.smallFontSizes: Font sizes below 12px are difficult to read on mobile devices and can fail accessibility standards. Users may need to pinch-to-zoom to read tiny text, which degrades the browsing experience.
whatIsIt.themeColorSet: Your theme color meta tag is set, allowing browsers and mobile devices to use your brand color in the address bar and task switcher.
whatIsIt.appleMobileConfigured: Apple mobile web app meta tags are configured, optimizing how your site appears when saved to the home screen on iOS devices.
whatIsIt.smallTapTargets: Buttons and links that are too small (under 44x44 pixels) are difficult to tap accurately on touch devices. Users may accidentally tap the wrong element, causing frustration.
whatIsIt.jsConsoleErrors: JavaScript errors detected on this page indicate bugs in your code that may break interactive features, forms, buttons, or other dynamic functionality for your visitors.
whatIsIt.brokenResources: Broken resources (404 errors) means some files (images, scripts, stylesheets) referenced on this page no longer exist. This can cause missing images, broken layouts, or non-functional features.
whatIsIt.redirectChain: A redirect chain occurs when a URL goes through two or more redirects before reaching its final destination (e.g., A → B → C). Each extra hop adds latency and dilutes link equity, hurting both user experience and SEO rankings.
whatIsIt.redirectLoop: A redirect loop occurs when two or more URLs redirect to each other in an infinite cycle (e.g., A → B → A). The browser gives up after several attempts and shows an error, making the page completely inaccessible to both users and search engines.
whatIsIt.brokenInternalLink: A broken internal link is a hyperlink on your site that points to another page on the same domain which returns a 404 (Not Found) error. This creates dead ends for users and wastes crawl budget, as search engines follow the link only to find nothing.
whatIsIt.serverErrors: Server errors (5xx responses) indicate problems on your hosting server. These errors prevent resources from loading properly and may intermittently affect page functionality.
whatIsIt.pageLoadFailed: The page could not be loaded for analysis. This could indicate a server error, redirect loop, authentication requirement, or other issue preventing access.
whatIsIt.psiScoreLow: Google PageSpeed Insights gave this page a poor performance score (below 50). This means the page loads slowly and provides a poor user experience, which can directly hurt search rankings.
whatIsIt.psiScoreModerate: Google PageSpeed Insights gave this page a moderate performance score (50-89). While functional, there is room for improvement to provide a faster, smoother user experience.
whatIsIt.psiScoreGood: Google PageSpeed Insights gave this page a good performance score (90+), indicating fast loading times and a good user experience.
whatIsIt.lcpPoor: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures when the biggest visible element finishes loading. A poor LCP (over 4 seconds) means users wait a long time before the main content appears, which frustrates visitors and hurts rankings.
whatIsIt.lcpNeedsWork: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) measures main content load time. An LCP over 2.5 seconds means the largest visible element takes longer than recommended to appear, affecting user perception of speed.
whatIsIt.lcpGood: Your Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) is within Google's recommended threshold, meaning the main content appears quickly for visitors.
whatIsIt.clsPoor: Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) measures visual stability. A poor CLS (over 0.25) means elements shift noticeably as the page loads, potentially causing users to click the wrong thing or lose their reading position.
whatIsIt.clsNeedsWork: Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) measures how much content moves during loading. A CLS over 0.1 means visible elements shift enough to be noticeable, slightly degrading the browsing experience.
whatIsIt.clsGood: Your Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) is low, meaning the page is visually stable and content doesn't jump around during loading.
whatIsIt.inpPoor: Interaction to Next Paint (INP) measures how quickly your page responds to user interactions (clicks, taps, key presses). A poor INP (over 500ms) means the page feels sluggish and unresponsive.
whatIsIt.inpNeedsWork: Interaction to Next Paint (INP) measures responsiveness. An INP over 200ms means interactions feel slightly delayed, which can affect user satisfaction with your site.
whatIsIt.inpGood: Your Interaction to Next Paint (INP) is good, meaning the page feels responsive and interactions are processed quickly.
whatIsIt.a11yImageAlt: Images must have alternative text (alt attribute) so that screen readers can describe them to visually impaired users. Alt text also displays when images fail to load and helps search engines understand image content.
whatIsIt.a11yInputImageAlt: Image buttons (<input type="image">) must have alt text to describe their function. Without it, screen readers cannot convey the button's purpose to keyboard and screen reader users.
whatIsIt.a11yAreaAlt: Clickable areas in image maps (<area> elements) need alt text so screen reader users can understand what each area links to or does.
whatIsIt.a11yColorContrast: Text must have sufficient color contrast against its background to be readable by users with low vision or color blindness. WCAG requires a minimum contrast ratio of 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text.
whatIsIt.a11yLinkName: Links must have discernible text that describes their destination. Screen reader users navigate by link text, so links like 'click here' or empty links are confusing and unusable.
whatIsIt.a11yButtonName: Buttons must have accessible text that describes their action. Screen readers announce this text to users, so buttons without names are effectively invisible to assistive technology users.
whatIsIt.a11yLabel: Form inputs must have labels so users know what information to enter. Labels are essential for screen readers and also improve usability for sighted users by providing clickable targets.
whatIsIt.a11yHtmlHasLang: The HTML document must have a lang attribute so screen readers can use the correct pronunciation rules and browsers can offer translation services.
whatIsIt.a11yValidLang: The lang attribute value must be a valid BCP 47 language tag. An invalid value may cause screen readers to use wrong pronunciation rules.
whatIsIt.a11yHtmlLangValid: The lang attribute on the HTML element must contain a valid language code. Invalid codes can cause assistive technologies to use incorrect speech modules.
whatIsIt.a11yDocumentTitle: The page must have a title that describes its topic or purpose. Screen readers announce the title when the page loads, helping users identify where they are.
whatIsIt.a11yRegion: All page content should be contained within landmark regions (header, main, nav, footer). Landmarks allow screen reader users to quickly navigate between major sections of the page.
whatIsIt.a11yHeadingOrder: Heading levels should increase by one level at a time (H1 to H2 to H3). Skipping levels confuses screen reader users who navigate by headings.
whatIsIt.a11yList: Lists must be structured correctly using proper HTML list elements (ul, ol, dl). Improperly structured lists confuse screen readers about the content's relationship.
whatIsIt.a11yListitem: List items (<li>) must be contained within a parent list element (<ul> or <ol>). Orphaned list items can confuse assistive technologies.
whatIsIt.a11yAriaAllowedAttr: ARIA attributes used must be appropriate for the element's role. Invalid ARIA attributes can cause screen readers to misrepresent the element's purpose and behavior.
whatIsIt.a11yAriaRequiredAttr: Elements with ARIA roles must have all required ARIA attributes. Missing required attributes prevent screen readers from properly communicating the element's state and properties.
whatIsIt.a11yAriaValidAttr: ARIA attribute values must be valid according to the specification. Invalid values may cause screen readers to announce incorrect information.
whatIsIt.a11yMetaViewport: The viewport meta tag must not disable user scaling. Users with low vision depend on zooming to enlarge content, and disabling it creates a significant accessibility barrier.
whatIsIt.a11yBypass: Pages must provide a way to skip repetitive navigation and jump directly to the main content. This is critical for keyboard-only users who would otherwise have to tab through every navigation link.
whatIsIt.a11yFrameTitle: Frames and iframes must have a title attribute that describes their content. Screen readers use this title to help users understand what the embedded content contains.
whatIsIt.a11yLandmarkOneMain: The page should have exactly one main landmark (<main> element) to identify the primary content area. This helps screen reader users quickly find the most important content.

howToFix.siteUnreachable: Check that your website URL is correct and the server is running. Verify DNS settings are configured properly. If the site was recently deployed, check with your hosting provider for any outages or configuration issues.
howToFix.auditFailed: Try running the audit again. If the problem persists, check if your website has any unusual configurations (e.g., aggressive rate limiting, CAPTCHA, or IP blocking) that may prevent the audit tool from accessing it.
howToFix.ttfbCritical: Optimize your server response time by enabling server-side caching, using a CDN (Content Delivery Network), upgrading to faster hosting, and optimizing database queries. Consider using a caching plugin if you're on WordPress.
howToFix.ttfbSlow: Improve server response time by enabling a caching layer (e.g., Redis, Varnish), using a CDN, optimizing slow database queries, and ensuring your hosting plan meets your traffic needs.
howToFix.htmlTooLarge: Reduce HTML size by removing unnecessary comments, whitespace, and unused code. Minify HTML output, move inline styles and scripts to external files, and consider lazy-loading content that isn't immediately visible.
howToFix.htmlLarge: Minimize HTML by removing comments and whitespace, moving inline scripts and styles to external files, and deferring non-critical content. Consider server-side minification.
howToFix.tooManyInlineScripts: Move inline JavaScript to external .js files that can be cached by browsers. Group related code into modules, and use modern bundling tools to combine scripts efficiently.
howToFix.tooManyScripts: Reduce external scripts by removing unused ones, combining multiple scripts into bundles, loading non-critical scripts asynchronously (async) or deferred (defer), and removing redundant tracking or analytics tools.
howToFix.manyScripts: Review your scripts and remove any that are unused. Consider bundling multiple scripts into fewer files, and use async or defer attributes for non-critical scripts to prevent render blocking.
howToFix.tooManyStylesheets: Combine multiple CSS files into fewer bundles. Remove unused stylesheets, load non-critical CSS asynchronously, and consider inlining critical CSS for above-the-fold content.
howToFix.imagesNotLazy: Add loading="lazy" to images that appear below the fold (not visible on initial load). Keep loading="eager" or no attribute for above-the-fold images like your hero image or logo.
howToFix.imagesNoDimensions: Add explicit width and height attributes to your tags. This allows the browser to reserve the correct space before the image loads, preventing layout shifts. Use CSS for responsive sizing.
howToFix.imagesNotNextGen: Convert your images to WebP or AVIF format using tools like Squoosh, Sharp, or your image CDN. Use the <picture> element with <source> tags to serve next-gen formats while keeping a JPEG/PNG fallback for older browsers.
howToFix.imagesTooLarge: Compress images using tools like TinyPNG, Squoosh, or ImageOptim. Resize images to the actual display dimensions — don't serve a 4000px image for a 800px container. Consider using an image CDN that auto-optimizes on delivery.
howToFix.imagesLargeWarning: Review images over 200KB and compress them further. Use appropriate quality settings (80-85% for JPEG is usually sufficient). Resize to actual display dimensions and consider using responsive images with srcset.
howToFix.noCompression: Enable gzip or brotli compression on your web server. For Apache, enable mod_deflate. For Nginx, add gzip directives. Most hosting providers and CDNs support enabling compression through their control panel.
howToFix.noCacheHeaders: Add Cache-Control headers to your server configuration. Set long cache durations for static assets (images, CSS, JS) and shorter durations for HTML. Use file versioning to bust stale caches when files change.
howToFix.renderBlockingCSS: Load non-critical CSS asynchronously using media attributes or JavaScript. Inline critical above-the-fold CSS in the HTML head. Consider using a critical CSS extraction tool to automate this.
howToFix.noTitle: Add a <title> tag inside the <head> section of your HTML. Keep it between 30-60 characters, descriptive of the page content, and include your primary keyword. Each page should have a unique title.
howToFix.titleTooShort: Expand your page title to at least 30 characters. Include relevant keywords and a clear description of what the page is about. Consider adding your brand name at the end.
howToFix.titleTooLong: Shorten your page title to 60 characters or fewer. Focus on the most important keywords and information first. Use a pipe (|) or dash (-) to separate the page topic from your brand name.
howToFix.noMetaDescription: Add a <meta name="description"> tag in the <head>. Write a compelling 120-160 character summary that includes relevant keywords and encourages users to click through from search results.
howToFix.metaDescriptionShort: Expand your meta description to at least 120 characters. Include relevant keywords naturally and write a compelling summary that encourages searchers to click.
howToFix.metaDescriptionLong: Shorten your meta description to 160 characters or fewer. Focus on the most important information and keywords first, as the end will be cut off in search results.
howToFix.noH1: Add a single H1 heading to your page that clearly describes the main topic. It should be the most prominent heading on the page. Include your primary keyword naturally in the H1 text.
howToFix.multipleH1: Keep only one H1 tag per page as the main heading. Convert additional H1 tags to H2 or lower-level headings. Use H2-H6 elements to create a clear hierarchical content structure.
howToFix.noH2: Add H2 headings to break your content into logical sections. This creates a clear content hierarchy that helps both readers and search engines understand your page structure.
howToFix.noCanonical: Add a <link rel="canonical" href="..."> tag in the <head> pointing to the preferred URL for this page. This prevents duplicate content issues when the same page is accessible via multiple URLs.
howToFix.missingOG: Add Open Graph meta tags in the <head>: og:title (page title), og:description (summary), and og:image (preview image URL, at least 1200x630px). This ensures attractive previews when your page is shared on social media.
howToFix.imagesNoAlt: Add descriptive alt text to each image using the alt attribute. Describe what the image shows concisely. Use empty alt="" for purely decorative images. Good alt text helps SEO and accessibility.
howToFix.noStructuredData: Add JSON-LD structured data in a <script type="application/ld+json"> tag. Use appropriate schema types (Article, Product, LocalBusiness, FAQ, etc.) from schema.org to describe your content.
howToFix.noLangAttribute: Add a lang attribute to your HTML tag (e.g., <html lang="en">). Use the correct ISO 639-1 language code for your content. For multilingual sites, set the lang on each page accordingly.
howToFix.fewInternalLinks: Add more internal links to other relevant pages on your site. Include links in your content, navigation, sidebar, or footer. Aim for at least 3-5 internal links per page to improve discoverability.
howToFix.noFavicon: Add a favicon by placing a favicon.ico file in your site's root directory, or use a <link rel="icon" href="..."> tag in the <head>. Create icons in multiple sizes (16x16, 32x32, 180x180) for different devices.
howToFix.metaRobotsNoindex: Remove the noindex meta tag or X-Robots-Tag header unless you intentionally want to hide this page from search engines. Check for <meta name="robots" content="noindex"> in your HTML or HTTP headers.
howToFix.metaRobotsNofollow: Remove the nofollow directive from your robots meta tag unless intentional. Check for <meta name="robots" content="nofollow"> in your HTML. You can keep nofollow on specific individual links if needed.
howToFix.robotsTxtBlocksAll: Edit your robots.txt file and change 'Disallow: /' to allow crawling. You can keep specific directories blocked if needed, but removing the blanket disallow rule lets search engines index your public pages.
howToFix.wpSearchEngineDiscouraged: In your WordPress dashboard, go to Settings > Reading and uncheck 'Discourage search engines from indexing this site.' Alternatively, use the Fix button on this page to change the setting remotely.
howToFix.noRobotsTxt: Create a robots.txt file in your site's root directory. Include basic directives like 'User-agent: *' and 'Allow: /'. Add your sitemap URL with 'Sitemap: https://yoursite.com/sitemap.xml'.
howToFix.robotsTxtError: Check that your robots.txt file is accessible at your-site.com/robots.txt. Verify there are no server errors blocking access, and ensure the file contains valid syntax.
howToFix.sitemapInvalid: Verify your sitemap.xml follows the correct XML sitemap protocol. Ensure proper XML headers, no broken URLs, and valid date formats. Use an online sitemap validator to identify specific issues.
howToFix.noSitemap: Create an XML sitemap listing all important pages on your site. If using WordPress, install an SEO plugin (e.g., Yoast, Rank Math) that auto-generates sitemaps. Submit it to Google Search Console.
howToFix.sitemapError: Check that your sitemap is accessible at the expected URL (usually /sitemap.xml). Verify the server returns it with the correct content type and there are no access restrictions.
howToFix.noHttps: Install an SSL certificate on your server and configure all traffic to redirect from HTTP to HTTPS. Most hosting providers offer free SSL certificates through Let's Encrypt. Update all internal links to use HTTPS.
howToFix.noHsts: Add the Strict-Transport-Security header to your server responses. For Apache, add it in .htaccess. For Nginx, add it in your server block. Start with a short max-age and increase it once confirmed working.
howToFix.noXFrameOptions: Add the X-Frame-Options header to your server responses. Use 'DENY' to prevent all framing, or 'SAMEORIGIN' to allow only your own site to frame your pages.
howToFix.noContentTypeOptions: Add 'X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff' to your server responses. This is a simple one-line header that prevents browsers from MIME-sniffing, closing a common attack vector.
howToFix.noCsp: Add a Content-Security-Policy header to define which sources can load scripts, styles, images, and other resources. Start with a report-only policy to test, then enforce it once confirmed.
howToFix.noReferrerPolicy: Add a 'Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin' header to your server responses. This balances privacy and functionality by sharing only the origin for cross-origin requests.
howToFix.noPermissionsPolicy: Add a Permissions-Policy header to control browser feature access. Disable features you don't use (e.g., camera, microphone) and explicitly allow only the features your site needs.
howToFix.mixedContent: Replace all HTTP resource URLs with HTTPS equivalents. Search your HTML, CSS, and JavaScript for 'http://' URLs and update them to 'https://'. Ensure external resources also support HTTPS.
howToFix.noViewport: Add <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1"> to the <head> of your HTML. This tells mobile browsers to set the viewport width to the device width and use a 1:1 scale.
howToFix.viewportNoDeviceWidth: Update your viewport meta tag to include width=device-width. The correct format is: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">.
howToFix.zoomDisabled: Remove user-scalable=no and maximum-scale=1 from your viewport meta tag. Users should always be able to zoom for accessibility. Keep width=device-width and initial-scale=1.
howToFix.fixedWidthElements: Replace fixed pixel widths with relative units like percentages (%), viewport units (vw), or flexible layout properties (flex, grid). Use CSS max-width instead of width for containers.
howToFix.noResponsiveImages: Add srcset and sizes attributes to your tags to serve different image sizes for different screen widths. Alternatively, use the element for art-direction responsive images.
howToFix.smallFontSizes: Increase font sizes to at least 12px minimum, with 16px recommended for body text. Use relative units (rem, em) instead of fixed pixels to respect user font-size preferences.
howToFix.smallTapTargets: Increase the size of clickable elements (buttons, links) to at least 44x44 pixels. Add padding to small links, increase button sizes, and ensure adequate spacing between adjacent tap targets.
howToFix.jsConsoleErrors: Open your browser's developer console (F12) to identify the specific JavaScript errors. Fix syntax errors, missing dependencies, and broken references. Test across different browsers.
howToFix.brokenResources: Check your HTML for references to files that return 404 errors. Update URLs to the correct paths, re-upload missing files, or remove references to resources that no longer exist.
howToFix.redirectChain: Identify all intermediate redirect hops using a redirect checker tool. Update your links, .htaccess rules, or redirect plugins so each URL points directly to the final destination, eliminating unnecessary hops.
howToFix.redirectLoop: Check your .htaccess file, redirect plugin rules, and server configuration for conflicting redirect rules. Remove circular redirects and ensure each rule points to a final, non-redirecting URL. Test the fix with a redirect checker.
howToFix.fixBrokenInternalLink: Option 1: Update the link's href to point to a valid page. Option 2: Set up a 301 redirect from the missing URL to the most relevant existing page. Option 3: Remove the broken link from your content entirely.
howToFix.serverErrors: Investigate server error logs to identify the cause of 5xx errors. Common causes include misconfigured server settings, exhausted resources, or application bugs. Contact your hosting provider if needed.
howToFix.pageLoadFailed: Verify the page URL is correct and accessible. Check for redirect loops, authentication requirements, or server errors. Ensure the page doesn't require special access to load.
howToFix.psiScoreLow: Focus on the highest-impact improvements: optimize images (WebP format, proper sizing), reduce JavaScript bundle size, enable caching, use a CDN, and minimize render-blocking resources.
howToFix.psiScoreModerate: Review the specific opportunities listed in PageSpeed Insights. Common improvements include deferring non-critical JavaScript, optimizing image sizes, and enabling text compression.
howToFix.lcpPoor: Optimize LCP by preloading the hero image or largest element, using a CDN, reducing server response time, removing render-blocking resources, and ensuring the largest image uses optimal format and dimensions.
howToFix.lcpNeedsWork: Improve LCP by preloading critical resources, optimizing images (proper sizing, WebP format), using browser caching, and reducing time spent on server-side processing.
howToFix.clsPoor: Fix layout shifts by setting explicit width and height on all images and videos, using CSS aspect-ratio, preloading web fonts, and avoiding dynamic content injection above existing content.
howToFix.clsNeedsWork: Reduce layout shifts by adding dimensions to images, reserving space for ads and embeds, loading fonts with font-display: swap, and avoiding inserting content above the viewport after load.
howToFix.inpPoor: Improve interactivity by breaking up long JavaScript tasks, deferring non-essential scripts, using web workers for heavy processing, reducing DOM size, and minimizing excessive event listeners.
howToFix.inpNeedsWork: Improve interaction speed by reducing JavaScript execution time during interactions, debouncing input handlers, lazy-loading non-critical interactive components, and avoiding layout thrashing.
howToFix.a11yImageAlt: Add an alt attribute to each tag describing what the image shows. For decorative images, use an empty alt attribute (alt=""). Keep alt text concise but descriptive - usually under 125 characters.
howToFix.a11yInputImageAlt: Add an alt attribute to <input type="image"> elements describing the button's action (e.g., alt="Submit" or alt="Search"). The alt text should describe the action, not the image appearance.
howToFix.a11yAreaAlt: Add an alt attribute to each <area> element in your image map. The alt text should describe the destination or purpose of each clickable region.
howToFix.a11yColorContrast: Increase the contrast between text and background colors. Use darker text on light backgrounds or lighter text on dark backgrounds. Use a contrast checker tool to verify a ratio of at least 4.5:1 for normal text.
howToFix.a11yLinkName: Add descriptive text to links. Avoid generic phrases like 'click here.' If a link wraps an image, add alt text to the image. For icon-only links, add aria-label describing the destination.
howToFix.a11yButtonName: Add visible text or an aria-label to buttons. For icon-only buttons, add aria-label describing the action (e.g., aria-label="Close menu"). Ensure the label clearly conveys the button's purpose.
howToFix.a11yLabel: Add a <label> element associated with each input via the 'for' attribute matching the input's 'id'. Alternatively, use aria-label or aria-labelledby for inputs where visual labels aren't feasible.
howToFix.a11yHtmlHasLang: Add a lang attribute to the <html> tag with the appropriate language code (e.g., <html lang="en"> for English, <html lang="he"> for Hebrew). Set it to match the page's primary language.
howToFix.a11yValidLang: Verify your lang attribute uses a valid BCP 47 language tag. Common codes: 'en' (English), 'he' (Hebrew), 'es' (Spanish), 'fr' (French), 'de' (German), 'ar' (Arabic).
howToFix.a11yHtmlLangValid: Update the HTML lang attribute to use a valid language code from the BCP 47 standard. Remove any typos or non-standard values.
howToFix.a11yDocumentTitle: Add a descriptive <title> tag inside the <head> section. The title should clearly identify the page's content or purpose (e.g., 'Contact Us - Company Name').
howToFix.a11yRegion: Wrap your page content in appropriate landmark elements: <header> for site header, <nav> for navigation, <main> for primary content, <footer> for site footer, and <aside> for sidebars.
howToFix.a11yHeadingOrder: Restructure headings to follow a sequential order. Start with H1, then H2 for major sections, H3 for subsections within H2, and so on. Don't skip heading levels.
howToFix.a11yList: Use proper HTML list elements: <ul> for unordered lists, <ol> for numbered lists, <dl> for definition lists. Each list item should be wrapped in a <li> (or <dt>/<dd> for definition lists).
howToFix.a11yListitem: Ensure all <li> elements are direct children of <ul> or <ol> elements. If you have orphaned list items, wrap them in the appropriate list container.
howToFix.a11yAriaAllowedAttr: Review ARIA attributes on your elements and ensure they match the element's role. Remove any ARIA attributes that are not allowed for the given role. Consult the WAI-ARIA specification.
howToFix.a11yAriaRequiredAttr: Add the missing required ARIA attributes for each element's role. For example, role="checkbox" requires aria-checked; role="slider" requires aria-valuenow, aria-valuemin, aria-valuemax.
howToFix.a11yAriaValidAttr: Fix ARIA attribute values to match the expected types. Boolean attributes should be 'true' or 'false.' ID references must point to existing elements. Check the WAI-ARIA specification for correct values.
howToFix.a11yMetaViewport: Remove user-scalable=no and maximum-scale=1.0 from your viewport meta tag. Keep width=device-width and initial-scale=1, but allow users to zoom in for accessibility.
howToFix.a11yBypass: Add a 'Skip to main content' link as the first focusable element on the page. It should be visible on focus and link to an anchor on the <main> element.
howToFix.a11yFrameTitle: Add a descriptive title attribute to all <iframe> and <frame> elements. The title should describe the content of the frame (e.g., title="Google Maps location").
howToFix.a11yLandmarkOneMain: Add a single <main> element to wrap your page's primary content. If you have multiple <main> elements, consolidate them into one. Use <section> or <div> for other content areas.
